message.content:
Task id: T73 (ledgerRefs goals:G5)
Base commit: 8c0411d (main) — FIRST run `git reset --hard 8c0411d` inside YOUR worktree (sanctioned base-refresh), then `git switch -c implement/T73`.

Go repo (wanbond). Gate: `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./internal/device/... -race && go test ./...` via `nix develop -c`. Fetch the FULL task text from the ledger (fetch_item tasks T73) — treat it as your spec.

CONTEXT: T70 landed hubFailover's mutable spec-keyed endpoint set with updateResolution(specIdx, addrs) (boot adoption from empty, D32 no-op suppression, dwell re-arm). T72 landed the [dns] config block (config.DNS with Resolver mode, PollInterval, Timeout, NewResolver()). internal/dnsresolve has the Resolver seam + System/DoH/DoT impls + FakeResolver.

ALSO RESOLVE DEFECT D46 while you're here (it is pinned to your task's contract): "hubFailover: stale active with flattened total < 2 strands the bond despite one live standby record" — the recommended fix is the T73-side policy: NEVER publish an empty expansion for a previously-resolved hostname (retain last-good records on lookup failure/NXDOMAIN/empty), which your tolerant-model requirement already implies. Make that retention explicit and tested.

headline: Implement the re-resolution controller: poll plus liveness-loss triggers, change-suppressed repoint

Summary of the ledger spec: new controller in internal/device (e.g. resolution.go) mirroring the hubFailover shape — pure constructor over injected collaborators (dnsresolve.Resolver, the update API, the hubHealth liveness plane, telemetry.Clock, poll interval from [dns]); per tick: for each hostname spec, Lookup with the configured timeout; on SUCCESS family-filter/order and call updateResolution (change detection inside); on FAILURE keep the previous expansion and retry next tick (never tear down a working set, never hard-fail); liveness-loss trigger: all paths to the ACTIVE endpoint DOWN → immediate out-of-band re-resolve of the active spec; coordinate with hubFailover purely through the shared lock + update API; resolution entirely off the send hot path; TTL clamp: next poll = min(pollInterval, TTL) when ttlOk; runs even for a single-hostname peer.

acceptance: go test ./internal/device/... -race passes with injected resolver + fake clock: (1) lookup failure at every tick leaves the endpoint set untouched and keeps retrying (no hard fail); (2) a changed active IP produces exactly one SetPeerRemote (observed via fake remote); (3) an unchanged IP produces none over many ticks; (4) all-paths-down triggers a re-resolve before the next poll tick; (5) TTL below poll interval shortens the next resolve delay. PLUS (D46): a lookup returning empty/NXDOMAIN for a previously-resolved hostname retains the last-good expansion (never publishes empty).

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

4		"net/netip"

16	// hubFailoverSettle is the dwell a newly-selected concentrator endpoint is given to
17	// prove itself LIVE before the controller may advance again. It exists to keep a
18	// STILL-DOWN reading — caused merely by echoes not having returned from the just-
19	// selected hub yet — from skipping straight past a healthy standby.
20	//
21	// It must comfortably exceed the liveness UP-recovery latency: a DOWN path comes UP
22	// after telemetry.DefaultUpSuccesses (3) probe echoes at telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval
23	// (200ms) ≈ 600ms once probes reach a reachable hub. At 3s the dwell clears that with a
24	// wide margin for jitter/RTT, and also bounds the re-advance cadence while a whole hub
25	// fleet is down to at most one switch (one fresh handshake) per 3s. Hub loss is a
26	// coarse, rare event (a concentrator down), distinct from the sub-second PER-PATH
27	// failover the schedulers own, so a switch latency on this scale is acceptable.
28	const hubFailoverSettle = 3 * time.Second
29	
30	// hubHealth is one path's liveness verdict, the read side of the T13 telemetry state
31	// machine (*telemetry.Prober satisfies it). The hub-failover controller reads exactly
32	// the same up/down verdict the schedulers select on, so "hub loss" is derived from the
33	// very liveness plane per-path failover already runs on — no second detector.
34	type hubHealth interface {
35		State() telemetry.PathState
36	}
37	
38	// peerRemote repoints the whole bond's wire remote at a new concentrator endpoint
39	// (*bind.Multipath satisfies it via SetPeerRemote). It is the action half of a hub
40	// switch on the DATA/PROBE plane; the re-handshake is separate (rehandshake).
41	type peerRemote interface {
42		SetPeerRemote(ap netip.AddrPort)
43	}
44	
45	// rehandshake drops the current WireGuard session and initiates a FRESH one against the
46	// peer's static key — no hub-to-hub state handoff. The device wires it to the engine
47	// peer (expire keypairs + send handshake initiation); a test injects a counter.
48	type rehandshake func()
49	
50	// hubFailover is the edge-side active-standby concentrator failover controller (Q18/T57).
51	// It watches the per-path liveness plane and, when EVERY path to the currently-active
52	// concentrator is DOWN simultaneously (HUB LOSS — distinct from a single path failing,
53	// which the schedulers already absorb), advances to the next endpoint in the ordered
54	// concentrator list, repoints every path's remote at it, and initiates a fresh WG
55	// re-handshake. It re-arms against the new endpoint and, if that one is also fully down,
56	// advances again.
57	//
58	// End-of-list policy: WRAP (round-robin modulo the list length). Once the last standby
59	// is exhausted the controller cycles back to index 0 and keeps retrying every endpoint
60	// in order. Wrap is chosen over stop because it preserves availability — a concentrator
61	// that RECOVERS earlier in the list is retried and settled on within one full cycle,
62	// whereas stopping at the last endpoint would strand the edge on a dead hub even after
63	// endpoint 0 came back. The settle dwell bounds the churn to one switch (one handshake)
64	// per hubFailoverSettle while the whole fleet is down, so the round-robin is a slow,
65	// bounded retry, not a storm.
66	//
67	// GUARD (must-hold invariant): a SINGLE-endpoint list takes NO failover action at all —
68	// check returns immediately, so the remote is never repointed and no re-handshake is
69	// initiated. A one-concentrator deployment therefore behaves EXACTLY as it did before
70	// T57 (the endpoints-list "list" is the one-element normalization of the legacy single
71	// endpoint form, per T54).
72	type hubFailover struct {
73		// specs is the ORDERED concentrator endpoint set, one failoverSpec per configured
74		// endpoint entry (config.Peer.EndpointSpecs), in TOML order. Each spec OWNS its
75		// current expansion ([]netip.AddrPort): a literal is a fixed single entry; a hostname
76		// is its latest resolved record set (empty until the resolver — wired by a later task —
77		// first calls updateResolution). The FLATTENED concatenation of every spec's expansion,
78		// in spec order, is the failover order; the flattened index is what `check` advances.
79		// The set is MUTABLE: updateResolution swaps a spec's expansion in place under mu.
80		specs []failoverSpec
81		// health is the per-path liveness, one entry per configured path. Immutable after
82		// construction (the prober set is fixed for the tunnel's life).
83		health []hubHealth
84	
85		remote      peerRemote
86		rehandshake rehandshake
87		clock       telemetry.Clock
88		// settle is the dwell a freshly-selected endpoint gets before another advance is
89		// allowed (hubFailoverSettle in production; a test injects its own).
90		settle time.Duration
91		log    log.Logger
92	
93		mu sync.Mutex
94		// activeSpec and activeAddr are the SPEC-SCOPED identity of the ACTIVE endpoint (R70):
95		// the pair (owning spec index, AddrPort) — NEVER a bare flattened index and NEVER a
96		// bare AddrPort. Because a hostname may legitimately re-resolve onto the SAME AddrPort
97		// as another spec's literal (T67's load-time duplicate check is textual host:port
98		// only), duplicate AddrPort VALUES may appear across different specs in the flattened
99		// list; a bare-value re-map could silently bind the active pointer to the wrong spec's
100		// entry, so the active endpoint is addressed by (specIdx, AddrPort). activeSpec == -1
101		// means no active entry yet (every spec's expansion is empty). Guarded by mu.
102		activeSpec int
103		activeAddr netip.AddrPort
104		// idx is the ACTIVE endpoint's position in the FLATTENED list, DERIVED from
105		// (activeSpec, activeAddr) and re-mapped after every mutation. It is a read-only cache
106		// for observation/logging; the source of truth is the spec-scoped identity above. -1
107		// when there is no active entry. Guarded by mu.
108		idx int
109		// lastSwitch is when the active endpoint was last (re)selected — initialized to
110		// construction time so endpoint 0 gets the SAME settle grace at boot that a switched
111		// endpoint gets, preventing a boot-time false failover while probers are still DOWN
112		// before the first echo returns. Guarded by mu.
113		lastSwitch time.Time
114	}
115	
116	// failoverSpec is one ordered endpoint entry together with its CURRENT expansion. A
117	// literal entry's expansion is the fixed single AddrPort; a hostname entry's expansion is
118	// its latest resolved record set (Q32 multi-record), swapped in place by updateResolution.
119	// The spec itself (config.EndpointSpec) is retained so the controller can tell a hostname
120	// spec — which may grow/shrink at runtime — from an immutable literal, e.g. when deciding
121	// whether the failover loop may ever have work to do.
122	type failoverSpec struct {
123		spec  config.EndpointSpec
124		addrs []netip.AddrPort
125	}
126	
127	// newHubFailover builds the controller over an ordered list of IP-LITERAL endpoints — the
128	// legacy all-literal form (each address becomes its own single-entry literal spec). It is
129	// a pure constructor (no goroutine, no engine dependency beyond the injected collaborators)
130	// so its decision logic is unit-testable against a fake clock, fake health, and fake
131	// remote/rehandshake.
132	func newHubFailover(endpoints []netip.AddrPort, health []hubHealth, remote peerRemote, rh rehandshake, clock telemetry.Clock, settle time.Duration, lg log.Logger) *hubFailover {
133		specs := make([]failoverSpec, len(endpoints))
134		for i, ap := range endpoints {
135			specs[i] = failoverSpec{spec: config.EndpointSpec{Addr: ap}, addrs: []netip.AddrPort{ap}}
136		}
137		return newHubFailoverFromSpecs(specs, health, remote, rh, clock, settle, lg)
138	}
139	
140	// newHubFailoverFromSpecs builds the controller over the ordered, spec-keyed endpoint set.
141	// The active endpoint is initialized to the FIRST flattened entry (spec order); when every
142	// spec's expansion is empty (e.g. a hostname-only peer before the resolver has run) there
143	// is no active entry yet (activeSpec == -1) and check takes no action until an expansion
144	// appears. Like newHubFailover it injects no goroutine or engine dependency.
145	func newHubFailoverFromSpecs(specs []failoverSpec, health []hubHealth, remote peerRemote, rh rehandshake, clock telemetry.Clock, settle time.Duration, lg log.Logger) *hubFailover {
146		h := &hubFailover{
147			specs:       specs,
148			health:      health,
149			remote:      remote,
150			rehandshake: rh,
151			clock:       clock,
152			settle:      settle,
153			lastSwitch:  clock.Now(),
154			log:         lg,
155		}
156		h.activeSpec, h.activeAddr = h.entryAtLocked(0)
157		h.idx = h.flatIndexLocked(h.activeSpec, h.activeAddr)
158		return h
159	}
160	
161	// flatLenLocked is the number of endpoints in the FLATTENED list (the sum of every spec's
162	// current expansion length). Caller holds mu.
163	func (h *hubFailover) flatLenLocked() int {
164		n := 0
165		for i := range h.specs {
166			n += len(h.specs[i].addrs)
167		}
168		return n
169	}
170	
171	// canFailoverLocked reports whether the controller could EVER take a failover action: the
172	// flattened list already holds >= 2 endpoints, or SOME spec is a hostname whose runtime
173	// resolution may yet grow it. An all-literal set with a single entry can never grow, so its
174	// loop is a pure no-op and need not run. Caller holds mu.
175	func (h *hubFailover) canFailoverLocked() bool {
176		if h.flatLenLocked() >= 2 {
177			return true
178		}
179		for i := range h.specs {
180			if h.specs[i].spec.IsName {
181				return true
182			}
183		}
184		return false
185	}
186	
187	// flatIndexLocked returns the position of (specIdx, addr) in the FLATTENED list, or -1 if
188	// no such entry exists. It matches BOTH the owning spec index AND the AddrPort — never the
189	// bare value — so a duplicate AddrPort contributed by a DIFFERENT spec is skipped (R70);
190	// within the owning spec the FIRST occurrence wins. Caller holds mu.
191	func (h *hubFailover) flatIndexLocked(specIdx int, addr netip.AddrPort) int {
192		flat := 0
193		for si := range h.specs {
194			for _, a := range h.specs[si].addrs {
195				if si == specIdx && a == addr {
196					return flat
197				}
198				flat++
199			}
200		}
201		return -1
202	}
203	
204	// entryAtLocked maps a FLATTENED index back to its (owning spec index, AddrPort), or
205	// (-1, zero) when flatIdx is out of range (including an empty flattened list). Caller holds mu.
206	func (h *hubFailover) entryAtLocked(flatIdx int) (int, netip.AddrPort) {
207		if flatIdx < 0 {
208			return -1, netip.AddrPort{}
209		}
210		n := 0
211		for si := range h.specs {
212			for _, a := range h.specs[si].addrs {
213				if n == flatIdx {
214					return si, a
215				}
216				n++
217			}
218		}
219		return -1, netip.AddrPort{}
220	}
221	
222	// containsAddrPort reports whether addrs holds target (exact AddrPort equality).
223	func containsAddrPort(addrs []netip.AddrPort, target netip.AddrPort) bool {
224		for _, a := range addrs {
225			if a == target {
226				return true
227			}
228		}
229		return false
230	}
231	
232	// updateResolution swaps specIdx's expansion under h.mu — the sole mutation point of the
233	// endpoint set — and reconciles the active pointer WITHIN its owning spec (R70 spec-scoped
234	// identity):
235	//   - BOOT ADOPTION (activeSpec == -1: every spec's expansion was empty, e.g. a hostname-
236	//     only peer before the resolver first ran): the first resolution that makes the
237	//     flattened list non-empty ADOPTS its head as the active endpoint — set (activeSpec,
238	//     activeAddr), point the bond via exactly ONE SetPeerRemote, one rehandshake, and ARM
239	//     the settle dwell (lastSwitch = now). Without this a single-hostname peer's bond would
240	//     never receive any endpoint: check cannot rescue it either, because a one-record
241	//     expansion keeps the flattened length at 1, permanently under check's total<2 guard.
242	//   - A STANDBY-only change (specIdx != activeSpec) NEVER touches the bond: the active
243	//     entry is untouched; only the DERIVED flattened idx is re-mapped (an earlier spec
244	//     growing/shrinking shifts the active entry's flattened position without moving it).
245	//   - When the ACTIVE spec's own expansion changes: if the active AddrPort SURVIVES the
246	//     swap within that spec, the active entry is stable — strictly NO repoint (Q31/D32
247	//     no-op suppression), only the idx re-maps. If the active AddrPort is GONE, the active
248	//     endpoint's IP has genuinely changed: repoint via exactly ONE SetPeerRemote (D32
249	//     disruptive → Rebaseline + rehandshake) and one rehandshake to the new expansion's
250	//     first entry, and RE-ARM the settle dwell (lastSwitch = now) so the freshly-repointed
251	//     endpoint gets its full grace before a subsequent all-down check may advance off it.
252	//
253	// A change that empties the active spec (nothing to point at) leaves the active identity
254	// stale — the flattened list no longer contains it — and the next check on hub loss
255	// advances off it; no repoint is issued to a non-existent endpoint.
256	func (h *hubFailover) updateResolution(specIdx int, addrs []netip.AddrPort) {
257		h.mu.Lock()
258		defer h.mu.Unlock()
259	
260		if specIdx < 0 || specIdx >= len(h.specs) {
261			return
262		}
263		h.specs[specIdx].addrs = addrs
264	
265		switch {
266		case h.activeSpec == -1:
267			// No active entry yet (every spec was empty at boot). If this resolution populated
268			// the flattened list, adopt its head, point the bond at it, and arm the settle dwell.
269			if adoptSpec, adoptAddr := h.entryAtLocked(0); adoptSpec != -1 {
270				h.activeSpec = adoptSpec
271				h.activeAddr = adoptAddr
272				h.lastSwitch = h.clock.Now()
273				h.remote.SetPeerRemote(adoptAddr)
274				if h.rehandshake != nil {
275					h.rehandshake()
276				}
277				h.log.Warn("hub failover: first endpoint resolution; adopted active concentrator endpoint and re-handshaked",
278					"spec_index", adoptSpec, "to_endpoint", adoptAddr.String())
279			}
280		case specIdx == h.activeSpec:
281			if !containsAddrPort(addrs, h.activeAddr) && len(addrs) > 0 {
282				// The active endpoint's own spec re-resolved off the current AddrPort: its IP
283				// changed. Repoint the bond once, re-handshake against the new first entry, and
284				// re-arm the settle dwell against the freshly-repointed endpoint.
285				prev := h.activeAddr
286				h.activeAddr = addrs[0]
287				h.lastSwitch = h.clock.Now()
288				h.remote.SetPeerRemote(h.activeAddr)
289				if h.rehandshake != nil {
290					h.rehandshake()
291				}
292				h.log.Warn("hub failover: active concentrator endpoint re-resolved; repointed remote and re-handshaked",
293					"spec_index", specIdx, "from_endpoint", prev.String(), "to_endpoint", h.activeAddr.String())
294			}
295			// Survived (or empty expansion): no repoint. idx re-maps below.
296		}
297	
298		h.idx = h.flatIndexLocked(h.activeSpec, h.activeAddr)
299	}
300	
301	// allDownLocked reports HUB LOSS: every path's liveness to the active concentrator is
302	// DOWN simultaneously. An empty health set is NOT hub loss (there is nothing to declare
303	// dead — a bind without the probe transport never drives this). Caller holds mu.
304	func (h *hubFailover) allDownLocked() bool {
305		if len(h.health) == 0 {
306			return false
307		}
308		for _, hp := range h.health {
309			if hp.State() != telemetry.StateDown {
310				return false
311			}
312		}
313		return true
314	}
315	
316	// check is one failover-evaluation step: on confirmed hub loss (and once the active
317	// endpoint's settle dwell has elapsed) it advances to the next endpoint, repoints the
318	// bond's remote, and initiates a fresh re-handshake. It is idempotent and cheap in the
319	// steady state (any path up, or a single-endpoint list) — a length check plus a liveness
320	// sweep — so a periodic loop may call it at the probe cadence.
321	func (h *hubFailover) check() {
322		h.mu.Lock()
323		defer h.mu.Unlock()
324	
325		// GUARD: a FLATTENED list of fewer than two endpoints takes NO failover action — no
326		// advance, no remote repoint, no re-handshake — so a one-concentrator deployment is
327		// byte-for-byte the pre-T57 behaviour, and a hostname peer whose resolution has not yet
328		// yielded a second endpoint waits rather than churning.
329		total := h.flatLenLocked()
330		if total < 2 {
331			return
332		}
333		if !h.allDownLocked() {
334			return
335		}
336		// Settle dwell: give the currently-active endpoint time for its liveness to recover
337		// before advancing, so a not-yet-echoed (transiently DOWN) healthy endpoint is not
338		// skipped. Applies at boot (endpoint 0) and after every switch.
339		now := h.clock.Now()
340		if now.Sub(h.lastSwitch) < h.settle {
341			return
342		}
343	
344		// Advance in FLATTENED order from the active entry's current flattened position. The
345		// position is re-derived from the spec-scoped identity every step, so a standby spec
346		// that grew/shrank since the last advance is walked at its new offset. A missing active
347		// (activeSpec == -1, or its AddrPort no longer present) resumes from the head.
348		prev := h.flatIndexLocked(h.activeSpec, h.activeAddr)
349		var nextIdx int
350		if prev < 0 {
351			nextIdx = 0
352		} else {
353			nextIdx = (prev + 1) % total
354		}
355		nextSpec, next := h.entryAtLocked(nextIdx)
356		h.activeSpec = nextSpec
357		h.activeAddr = next
358		h.idx = nextIdx
359		h.lastSwitch = now
360	
361		// Repoint the DATA/PROBE plane FIRST so probes immediately start re-arming detection
362		// against the new endpoint, then initiate the fresh WG re-handshake toward it.
363		h.remote.SetPeerRemote(next)
364		if h.rehandshake != nil {
365			h.rehandshake()
366		}
367		h.log.Warn("hub failover: all paths to active concentrator down; switched endpoint and re-handshaked",
368			"from_index", prev, "to_index", nextIdx, "to_spec", nextSpec, "to_endpoint", next.String(), "endpoints", total)
369	}
370	
371	// deviceRehandshake returns a rehandshake bound to the engine peer identified by pk: it
372	// EXPIRES the peer's current keypairs (dropping the old hub's session — a fresh session
373	// with NO hub-to-hub state handoff) and sends a fresh handshake initiation, which the
374	// Bind fans out to the just-repointed standby endpoint. All hubs in the ordered set
375	// share the peer's single WireGuard static key, so the SAME peer identity re-handshakes
376	// against whichever hub is now active. A peer the engine cannot resolve (never configured
377	// — impossible for the edge's sole peer) yields a no-op so the loop never dereferences
378	// nil. This is the ONLY engine-peer coupling the failover path takes; it stays in the
379	// device package alongside the rest of the engine wiring.
380	func deviceRehandshake(dev *awgdevice.Device, pk config.Key) rehandshake {
381		key := awgdevice.NoisePublicKey(pk.Bytes())
382		return func() {
383			peer := dev.LookupPeer(key)
384			if peer == nil {
385				return
386			}
387			// Drop the current session so the re-handshake starts a FRESH keypair (no hub-to-hub
388			// handoff); ExpireCurrentKeypairs also backdates lastSentHandshake so the immediately
389			// following initiation is not suppressed by the RekeyTimeout guard.
390			peer.ExpireCurrentKeypairs()
391			_ = peer.SendHandshakeInitiation(false)
392		}
393	}
394	
395	// peerNeedsHubFailover reports whether a peer's endpoint set warrants a hub-failover
396	// controller: it does when the peer carries ANY hostname spec (its runtime resolution may
397	// yield further endpoints, and even a single hostname can re-resolve onto a new IP the bond
398	// must be repointed at) OR when >= 2 IP-literal endpoints are configured (an ordered
399	// active-standby list). A single-IP-literal peer needs NO controller — it is byte-for-byte
400	// the pre-G5 single-concentrator deployment (Q29), so the failover path never constructs
401	// or runs for it.
402	func peerNeedsHubFailover(peer config.Peer) bool {
403		for _, s := range peer.EndpointSpecs {
404			if s.IsName {
405				return true
406			}
407		}
408		return len(peer.Endpoints) >= 2
409	}
410	
411	// startHubFailover builds and starts the edge-side hub-failover monitor for cfg's
412	// concentrator peer, or returns a NO-OP stopper when hub failover does not apply: the
413	// concentrator role (it roams edges dynamically and has no endpoint list), a bind without
414	// the probe transport (no probers → no liveness plane), or no peer whose endpoint set
415	// warrants a controller (peerNeedsHubFailover — a single-IP-literal deployment gets none,
416	// staying behaviour-identical to pre-G5). The edge bonds every path to a SINGLE
417	// concentrator, so the whole per-path prober set IS the liveness of the paths to that
418	// concentrator (hub loss = every one DOWN); the controller drives the first qualifying
419	// peer. The returned stopper is what device.Close invokes to halt the loop.
420	func startHubFailover(cfg *config.Config, mp *bind.Multipath, probers []*telemetry.Prober, dev *awgdevice.Device, lg log.Logger) func() {
421		if cfg.Role != config.RoleEdge || len(probers) == 0 {
422			return func() {}
423		}
424		for _, peer := range cfg.WireGuard.Peers {
425			if !peerNeedsHubFailover(peer) {
426				continue
427			}
428			health := make([]hubHealth, len(probers))
429			for i, pr := range probers {
430				health[i] = pr
431			}
432			// Seed each spec's expansion: a literal is its fixed single AddrPort; a hostname
433			// starts EMPTY — resolution is a later task's job (Q30), not wired here.
434			specs := make([]failoverSpec, len(peer.EndpointSpecs))
435			for i, s := range peer.EndpointSpecs {
436				var addrs []netip.AddrPort
437				if !s.IsName {
438					addrs = []netip.AddrPort{s.Addr}
439				}
440				specs[i] = failoverSpec{spec: s, addrs: addrs}
441			}
442			ctrl := newHubFailoverFromSpecs(
443				specs, health, mp,
444				deviceRehandshake(dev, peer.PublicKey),
445				telemetry.SystemClock{}, hubFailoverSettle,
446				lg.Component("hubfailover"),
447			)
448			// Poll at the probe cadence: check is cheap (a length check plus a liveness sweep)
449			// and the settle dwell bounds actual switches, so a responsive poll only tightens
450			// detection latency without churning the remote.
451			return ctrl.startHubFailoverLoop(telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval)
452		}
453		return func() {}
454	}
455	
456	// startHubFailoverLoop launches the failover-evaluation goroutine: it calls check every
457	// interval until the returned stopper is invoked (idempotent). It mirrors the bind's
458	// StartProbeLoop/StartReconcileLoop lifecycle glue — a wall-clock ticker driving a
459	// decision function whose every timing choice runs through the injected Clock, so a test
460	// drives check directly against a fake clock and never starts this goroutine. It is a
461	// no-op (no-op stopper) for a controller that can NEVER fail over (canFailoverLocked: an
462	// all-literal set with fewer than two entries — no hostname to grow it) or a non-positive
463	// interval. A hostname peer whose expansion is still empty DOES start the loop: its
464	// resolution may later populate a second endpoint, and check no-ops cheaply until then.
465	func (h *hubFailover) startHubFailoverLoop(interval time.Duration) (stop func()) {
466		h.mu.Lock()
467		can := h.canFailoverLocked()
468		h.mu.Unlock()
469		if !can || interval <= 0 {
470			return func() {}
471		}
472		done := make(chan struct{})
473		go func() {
474			ticker := time.NewTicker(interval)
475			defer ticker.Stop()
476			for {
477				select {
478				case <-done:
479					return
480				case <-ticker.C:
481					h.check()
482				}
483			}
484		}()
485		var once sync.Once
486		return func() { once.Do(func() { close(done) }) }
487	}
488

1	package dnsresolve

3	import (
4		"context"
5		"fmt"
6		"net/netip"

8	)
9	
10	// FakeResolver is a hand-written in-memory Resolver backed by a static
11	// host->addrs map (the dual-tests dummy for internal/dnsresolve): unit tests
12	// across the codebase inject it in place of SystemResolver.
13	type FakeResolver struct {
14		// Hosts maps a hostname to the addrs Lookup returns for it, in order.
15		Hosts map[string][]netip.Addr
16		// MinTTL and TTLOk are returned verbatim for every mapped host.
17		MinTTL time.Duration
18		TTLOk  bool
19	}
20	
21	var _ Resolver = (*FakeResolver)(nil)
22	
23	// Lookup implements Resolver: it returns the addrs mapped to host, or a
24	// non-nil error when host is not in Hosts.
25	func (f *FakeResolver) Lookup(ctx context.Context, host string) ([]netip.Addr, time.Duration, bool, error) {
26		if err := ctx.Err(); err != nil {
27			return nil, 0, false, err
28		}
29		addrs, ok := f.Hosts[host]
30		if !ok {
31			return nil, 0, false, fmt.Errorf("dnsresolve: no such host %q", host)
32		}
33		return addrs, f.MinTTL, f.TTLOk, nil
34	}

1	package telemetry
2	
3	import (
4		"time"
5	
6		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
7	)
8	
9	// Clock is the injectable time source the liveness machinery reads. Production
10	// code passes SystemClock; tests pass a hand-advanced fake so transitions are
11	// deterministic and instant.
12	type Clock interface {
13		Now() time.Time
14	}
15	
16	// SystemClock is the wall-clock Clock.
17	type SystemClock struct{}
18	
19	// Now returns the current wall-clock time.
20	func (SystemClock) Now() time.Time { return time.Now() }
21	
22	// PathState is the liveness verdict for one path. Path liveness is entirely this
23	// codec's concern: the inner WireGuard keepalive is per-peer, not per-path, so a
24	// dead uplink under a live peer is invisible to WireGuard and must be detected
25	// here.
26	type PathState uint8
27	
28	const (
29		// StateDown means no fresh probe echo has been observed within the detection
30		// threshold; the path is presumed unusable.
31		StateDown PathState = iota
32		// StateUp means the path has recently returned probe echoes.
33		StateUp
34	)
35	
36	// String renders the state as the lowercase token used in structured logs.
37	func (s PathState) String() string {
38		switch s {
39		case StateUp:
40			return "up"
41		case StateDown:
42			return "down"
43		default:
44			return "unknown"
45		}
46	}
47	
48	// LivenessConfig holds the up/down detection thresholds. DownAfter is the
49	// silence duration that marks an up path down; UpAfterSuccesses is the number of
50	// consecutive echoes required to bring a down path up. The asymmetry is the

1	package config
2	
3	import (
4		"errors"
5		"fmt"
6		"net"
7		"net/netip"
8		"net/url"
9		"strings"
10		"time"
11	
12		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/dnsresolve"
13	)
14	
15	// DNSResolverMode selects the transport dns.resolver picks.
16	type DNSResolverMode string
17	
18	const (
19		// DNSResolverSystem uses the OS stub resolver (dnsresolve.SystemResolver).
20		// It is the default when [dns] is omitted or resolver is left empty.
21		DNSResolverSystem DNSResolverMode = "system"
22		// DNSResolverDoH uses DNS-over-HTTPS (dnsresolve.DoHResolver).
23		DNSResolverDoH DNSResolverMode = "doh"
24		// DNSResolverDoT uses DNS-over-TLS (dnsresolve.DoTResolver).
25		DNSResolverDoT DNSResolverMode = "dot"
26	)
27	
28	func (m DNSResolverMode) valid() bool {
29		return m == DNSResolverSystem || m == DNSResolverDoH || m == DNSResolverDoT
30	}
31	
32	// Default cadence/timeout for the [dns] block (Q31): the poll interval sits on
33	// the reconcile-cadence scale — frequent enough that a DDNS repoint is picked
34	// up promptly, far coarser than the sub-second deferred-path/probe loops
35	// (bind.DefaultReconcileInterval = 1s) since re-resolution costs a real
36	// network round trip to the configured resolver and re-resolving too eagerly
37	// would needlessly load it. The per-lookup timeout mirrors dnsresolve's own
38	// internal DoH/DoT bounds (5s).
39	const (
40		defaultDNSPollInterval = 30 * time.Second
41		defaultDNSTimeout      = 5 * time.Second
42	)
43	
44	// dotDefaultPort mirrors dnsresolve's unexported dotPort: DNS-over-TLS's
45	// IANA-assigned port (RFC 7858 §3), and the ONLY port dnsresolve.NewDoTResolver
46	// ever dials (it appends this fixed port itself). An explicit port in
47	// dot_server must match it, so a mistyped non-standard port fails fast at
48	// config load rather than being silently ignored when the resolver is built.
49	const dotDefaultPort = "853"
50	
51	// DNS selects and tunes the resolver transport used for opted-in hostname
52	// peer-endpoint re-resolution (Q29/Q31). An ABSENT [dns] block is INERT: it
53	// defaults to the system resolver, and resolution only happens at all when a
54	// peer separately opts in with its own `dns = true` flag (Peer.DNS) —
55	// [dns] merely selects which transport that opt-in uses, it is never a gate
56	// by itself.
57	type DNS struct {
58		// Resolver selects the transport: "system" (default), "doh", or "dot".
59		Resolver DNSResolverMode `toml:"resolver"`
60		// DoHURL is the DNS-over-HTTPS query endpoint (an https:// URL). Required
61		// iff resolver = "doh"; rejected (must stay empty) under any other
62		// resolver.
63		DoHURL string `toml:"doh_url"`
64		// DoTServer is the DNS-over-TLS server: a bare host, or "host:port" where
65		// port must be dotDefaultPort (853) — dnsresolve.NewDoTResolver always
66		// dials that port itself, so any other explicit port cannot be honored.
67		// Required iff resolver = "dot"; rejected (must stay empty) under any
68		// other resolver.
69		DoTServer string `toml:"dot_server"`
70		// BootstrapIP is the literal IP address wanbond dials the DoH/DoT server
71		// on when doh_url/dot_server names it by HOSTNAME rather than IP literal
72		// (the chicken-and-egg BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant, Q33): resolving the
73		// private resolver's own name would itself require a DNS lookup — via
74		// the system resolver [dns] exists to avoid — defeating the point of
75		// configuring a private resolver. Required iff the doh_url/dot_server
76		// host is a hostname; rejected (must stay empty) when the host is
77		// already an IP literal — there is nothing to bootstrap in that case, so
78		// a non-empty value there is a mode mismatch, rejected fail-fast like the
79		// other stray-field checks in validate.
80		BootstrapIP string `toml:"bootstrap_ip"`
81		// PollInterval is the re-resolution cadence for an opted-in hostname peer
82		// endpoint (Q31), parsed from PollIntervalRaw in applyDefaults. Defaults to
83		// defaultDNSPollInterval when PollIntervalRaw is left empty; must be > 0
84		// after defaulting.
85		PollInterval time.Duration `toml:"-"`
86		// PollIntervalRaw is the TOML Go-duration string form of PollInterval,
87		// e.g. "30s". Mirrors Path.LinkRTTRaw: go-toml/v2 cannot decode a TOML
88		// string directly into a time.Duration field, so the raw string is parsed
89		// via time.ParseDuration in applyDefaults.
90		PollIntervalRaw string `toml:"poll_interval"`
91		// Timeout bounds a single resolver lookup, parsed from TimeoutRaw in
92		// applyDefaults. Defaults to defaultDNSTimeout when TimeoutRaw is left
93		// empty; must be > 0 after defaulting.
94		Timeout time.Duration `toml:"-"`
95		// TimeoutRaw is the TOML Go-duration string form of Timeout, e.g. "5s".
96		TimeoutRaw string `toml:"timeout"`
97	}
98	
99	// applyDefaults fills the resolver mode and cadence/timeout knobs left at
100	// their zero value, so a minimal or absent [dns] block resolves to explicit,
101	// usable settings — an absent block still ends up as the system resolver
102	// with the standard cadence/timeout, per the type's zero-value contract.
103	// PollIntervalRaw/TimeoutRaw are parsed here (mirroring Path's *Raw-field
104	// normalize step for LinkRTTRaw); an unparseable duration string is reported
105	// immediately.
106	func (d *DNS) applyDefaults() error {
107		if d.Resolver == "" {
108			d.Resolver = DNSResolverSystem
109		}
110		if d.PollIntervalRaw == "" {
111			d.PollInterval = defaultDNSPollInterval
112		} else {
113			v, err := time.ParseDuration(d.PollIntervalRaw)
114			if err != nil {
115				return fmt.Errorf("dns.poll_interval: invalid duration %q: %w", d.PollIntervalRaw, err)
116			}
117			d.PollInterval = v
118		}
119		if d.TimeoutRaw == "" {
120			d.Timeout = defaultDNSTimeout
121		} else {
122			v, err := time.ParseDuration(d.TimeoutRaw)
123			if err != nil {
124				return fmt.Errorf("dns.timeout: invalid duration %q: %w", d.TimeoutRaw, err)
125			}
126			d.Timeout = v
127		}
128		return nil
129	}
130	
131	// validate enforces the [dns] block invariants: a resolver-mode-appropriate
132	// set of required fields, no stray fields left over from a different mode,
133	// positive cadence/timeout, and the BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant for a hostname-form
134	// doh_url/dot_server. Runs after applyDefaults (mirroring Amnezia/FEC/
135	// SchedulerConfig), so Resolver/PollInterval/Timeout are already defaulted.
136	func (d DNS) validate() error {
137		if !d.Resolver.valid() {
138			return fmt.Errorf("dns.resolver must be %q, %q or %q, got %q", DNSResolverSystem, DNSResolverDoH, DNSResolverDoT, d.Resolver)
139		}
140		if d.PollInterval <= 0 {
141			return fmt.Errorf("dns.poll_interval must be > 0, got %s", d.PollInterval)
142		}
143		if d.Timeout <= 0 {
144			return fmt.Errorf("dns.timeout must be > 0, got %s", d.Timeout)
145		}
146	
147		switch d.Resolver {
148		case DNSResolverSystem:
149			if d.DoHURL != "" {
150				return errors.New("dns.doh_url is only meaningful when dns.resolver = \"doh\"")
151			}
152			if d.DoTServer != "" {
153				return errors.New("dns.dot_server is only meaningful when dns.resolver = \"dot\"")
154			}
155			if d.BootstrapIP != "" {
156				return errors.New("dns.bootstrap_ip is only meaningful when dns.resolver is \"doh\" or \"dot\"")
157			}
158			return nil
159		case DNSResolverDoH:
160			if d.DoTServer != "" {
161				return errors.New("dns.dot_server is not meaningful when dns.resolver = \"doh\"")
162			}
163			if d.DoHURL == "" {
164				return errors.New("dns.doh_url is required when dns.resolver = \"doh\"")
165			}
166			host, err := dohURLHost(d.DoHURL)
167			if err != nil {
168				return fmt.Errorf("dns.doh_url: %w", err)
169			}
170			return d.requireBootstrapForHost(host)
171		case DNSResolverDoT:
172			if d.DoHURL != "" {
173				return errors.New("dns.doh_url is not meaningful when dns.resolver = \"dot\"")
174			}
175			if d.DoTServer == "" {
176				return errors.New("dns.dot_server is required when dns.resolver = \"dot\"")
177			}
178			host, err := dotServerHost(d.DoTServer)
179			if err != nil {
180				return fmt.Errorf("dns.dot_server: %w", err)
181			}
182			return d.requireBootstrapForHost(host)
183		default:
184			// Unreachable: d.Resolver.valid() above already rejected anything else.
185			return fmt.Errorf("dns.resolver: unhandled mode %q", d.Resolver)
186		}
187	}
188	
189	// requireBootstrapForHost enforces the BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant for the given
190	// (already-extracted) doh_url/dot_server host: an IP-literal host needs no
191	// bootstrap and rejects a stray bootstrap_ip as a mode mismatch (mirroring
192	// the doh_url/dot_server stray-field checks in validate — NewResolver would
193	// otherwise silently pin the dial to bootstrap_ip instead of the configured
194	// host); a hostname requires an explicit, valid bootstrap_ip.
195	func (d DNS) requireBootstrapForHost(host string) error {
196		if _, err := netip.ParseAddr(host); err == nil {
197			if d.BootstrapIP != "" {
198				return fmt.Errorf("dns.bootstrap_ip is not meaningful when the doh_url/dot_server host %q is already an IP literal", host)
199			}
200			return nil
201		}
202		if d.BootstrapIP == "" {
203			return fmt.Errorf("hostname %q requires dns.bootstrap_ip: the resolver's own address must be reachable without a DNS lookup (a plaintext lookup of the private resolver's own name would defeat the point of configuring one)", host)
204		}
205		if _, err := netip.ParseAddr(d.BootstrapIP); err != nil {
206			return fmt.Errorf("dns.bootstrap_ip %q is not a valid IP literal: %w", d.BootstrapIP, err)
207		}
208		return nil
209	}
210	
211	// dohURLHost extracts and validates the host component of a doh_url: it must
212	// be a well-formed https:// URL with a non-empty host.
213	func dohURLHost(rawURL string) (string, error) {
214		u, err := url.Parse(rawURL)
215		if err != nil {
216			return "", fmt.Errorf("invalid URL %q: %w", rawURL, err)
217		}
218		if u.Scheme != "https" {
219			return "", fmt.Errorf("must use https, got scheme %q in %q", u.Scheme, rawURL)
220		}
221		host := u.Hostname()
222		if host == "" {
223			return "", fmt.Errorf("missing host in %q", rawURL)
224		}
225		return host, nil
226	}
227	
228	// dotServerHost extracts and validates the host component of a dot_server: a
229	// bare host (hostname or IP literal, no port) is accepted as-is; a
230	// "host:port" form is split and requires port == dotDefaultPort, since
231	// dnsresolve.NewDoTResolver always dials that fixed port itself.
232	func dotServerHost(raw string) (string, error) {
233		if raw == "" {
234			return "", errors.New("empty")
235		}
236		// A bare IP literal (including an unbracketed IPv6 literal, which itself
237		// contains colons) has no port to split off.
238		if _, err := netip.ParseAddr(raw); err == nil {
239			return raw, nil
240		}
241		if !strings.Contains(raw, ":") {
242			return raw, nil // bare hostname, no port
243		}
244		host, port, err := net.SplitHostPort(raw)
245		if err != nil {
246			return "", fmt.Errorf("invalid dot_server %q: %w", raw, err)
247		}
248		if port != dotDefaultPort {
249			return "", fmt.Errorf("port %q must be %q (the IANA-assigned DNS-over-TLS port; dnsresolve always dials it)", port, dotDefaultPort)
250		}
251		return host, nil
252	}
253	
254	// NewResolver constructs the dnsresolve.Resolver implementation the
255	// (validated) [dns] block selects: dnsresolve.NewSystemResolver for the
256	// default/system mode, dnsresolve.NewDoHResolver/NewDoTResolver for doh/dot,
257	// or their *WithBootstrap variants when bootstrap_ip is set. Callers
258	// normally obtain a DNS value via config.Load, which already ran
259	// applyDefaults/validate; calling this on an un-validated DNS value surfaces
260	// whatever the underlying dnsresolve constructor rejects.
261	//
262	// bootstrap_ip (validated fail-fast, see requireBootstrapForHost) IS wired
263	// into the constructed resolver's dial target: when set, the resolver dials
264	// bootstrap_ip directly instead of resolving the configured hostname through
265	// the system dialer — the BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant (Q33) exists precisely to
266	// avoid that plaintext lookup of the private resolver's own name.
267	func (d DNS) NewResolver() (dnsresolve.Resolver, error) {
268		switch d.Resolver {
269		case DNSResolverDoH:
270			if d.BootstrapIP != "" {
271				return dnsresolve.NewDoHResolverWithBootstrap(d.DoHURL, d.BootstrapIP)
272			}
273			return dnsresolve.NewDoHResolver(d.DoHURL)
274		case DNSResolverDoT:
275			host, err := dotServerHost(d.DoTServer)
276			if err != nil {
277				return nil, fmt.Errorf("dns.dot_server: %w", err)
278			}
279			if d.BootstrapIP != "" {
280				return dnsresolve.NewDoTResolverWithBootstrap(host, d.BootstrapIP)
281			}
282			return dnsresolve.NewDoTResolver(host)
283		default:
284			return dnsresolve.NewSystemResolver(), nil
285		}
286	}

225	  WG session is untouched. This is the common case.
226	- **Hub failover** (this section): the *concentrator itself* is unreachable —
227	  **every** path's liveness to the active concentrator endpoint is DOWN
228	  simultaneously (HUB LOSS). No surviving uplink can reach it, so switching
229	  uplinks cannot help; the edge must move to a *standby concentrator*.
230	
231	An edge peer carries an **ordered** concentrator endpoint list
232	(`config.Peer.Endpoints`, Q18/T54): index 0 is the active/primary hub, the rest
233	are ordered standbys. Endpoints are **IP:port only — no DNS resolution** (the T54
234	constraint). All hubs in the set share the peer's **single WireGuard static key**,
235	so the same peer identity re-handshakes against whichever hub is active.
236	
237	The controller (`hubFailover`) runs a device-lifecycle monitor loop (started after
238	`dev.Up`, stopped before `dev.Close`, alongside the probe/reconcile loops):
239	
240	1. **Detect** hub loss off the **existing per-path liveness plane** — the same
241	   `telemetry.Prober` `State()` the schedulers select on — as *every* path
242	   reporting `StateDown`. No second detector.
243	2. **Advance** to the next endpoint in the ordered list and **repoint every
244	   path's remote** at it via `bind.Multipath.SetPeerRemote` (a uniform override —
245	   a hub switch retargets the whole bond; it supersedes any per-path `dest_addr`).
246	   This changes only the per-path fan-out *beneath* the engine's single virtual
247	   endpoint — **invariant A1 holds** (the engine still sees one peer, no
248	   per-packet endpoint churn).
249	3. **Re-handshake**: expire the peer's current keypairs (a **fresh** session —

WG session is untouched. This is the common case.
- **Hub failover** (this section): the *concentrator itself* is unreachable —
  **every** path's liveness to the active concentrator endpoint is DOWN
  simultaneously (HUB LOSS). No surviving uplink can reach it, so switching
  uplinks cannot help; the edge must move to a *standby concentrator*.

An edge peer carries an **ordered** concentrator endpoint list
(`config.Peer.Endpoints`, Q18/T54): index 0 is the active/primary hub, the rest
are ordered standbys. Endpoints are **IP:port only — no DNS resolution** (the T54
constraint). All hubs in the set share the peer's **single WireGuard static key**,
so the same peer identity re-handshakes against whichever hub is active.

The controller (`hubFailover`) runs a device-lifecycle monitor loop (started after
`dev.Up`, stopped before `dev.Close`, alongside the probe/reconcile loops):

1. **Detect** hub loss off the **existing per-path liveness plane** — the same
   `telemetry.Prober` `State()` the schedulers select on — as *every* path
   reporting `StateDown`. No second detector.
2. **Advance** to the next endpoint in the ordered list and **repoint every
   path's remote** at it via `bind.Multipath.SetPeerRemote` (a uniform override —
   a hub switch retargets the whole bond; it supersedes any per-path `dest_addr`).
   This changes only the per-path fan-out *beneath* the engine's single virtual
   endpoint — **invariant A1 holds** (the engine still sees one peer, no
   per-packet endpoint churn).
3. **Re-handshake**: expire the peer's current keypairs (a **fresh** session —
   **no hub-to-hub state handoff**) and send a fresh handshake initiation toward
   the just-repointed standby. This is the only engine-*peer* coupling the
   failover path takes; it lives in `internal/device` next to the rest of the
   engine wiring (the `conn`-seam isolation of `bind.go` is unaffected).

   hub's high-rate stream advanced the shared `reseq.Resequencer` to. Its first

   *suspect* branch and be dropped, because the unauthenticated-DATA resync guard
   needs several corroborating low seqs and a freshly re-handshaking standby emits
   only ~one DATA frame per `RekeyTimeout` — so corroboration falls outside the
   failover window and the tunnel never re-establishes. `SetPeerRemote` therefore
   calls `Resequencer.Rebaseline`: because a hub switch is a **trusted control
   event** (not a forgeable wire frame), the release point is re-anchored to the
   standby's *first* frame immediately, discarding the dead hub's buffered frames
   while leaving already-delivered frames untouched.
5. **Re-arm** against the new endpoint: probes now flow to it, so if it too is
   fully down the controller advances again.

**Settle dwell.** After a switch (and at boot for endpoint 0) the newly-selected
endpoint gets a fixed dwell (`hubFailoverSettle`, 3 s) to prove itself LIVE before
another advance is allowed. It comfortably exceeds the liveness UP-recovery latency
(~3 echoes × 200 ms ≈ 600 ms once probes reach a reachable hub), so a still-DOWN
reading caused merely by echoes not having returned yet cannot skip past a healthy
standby, and it bounds the re-advance cadence (one switch / one handshake per dwell)
while a whole hub fleet is down.

**End-of-list policy: WRAP** (round-robin modulo the list length). Once the last
standby is exhausted the controller cycles back to index 0 and keeps retrying every
endpoint in order. Wrap is chosen over *stop* to preserve availability — a hub that
recovers earlier in the list is retried and settled on within one cycle, whereas
stopping at the last endpoint would strand the edge on a dead hub even after
endpoint 0 came back. The settle dwell keeps the round-robin a slow, bounded retry,
not a storm.

**GUARD (must-hold invariant).** A **single-endpoint** list takes **no** failover
action — no advance, no remote repoint, no re-handshake. A one-concentrator
deployment (including the legacy single `endpoint` form, normalized to a
one-element list) is therefore byte-for-byte the pre-T57 behaviour. The switch and
this guard are validated by the real-network netns e2e (`TestHubFailoverStandbySwitch`
+ `TestHubFailoverSingleEndpointGuard`, T62) and, over the real internet, by the
realhosts mid-transfer WAN-kill tier (`TestRealMidTransferWANKill`, T63).

### Per-path telemetry — `internal/telemetry`

Measures per-path quality (RTT, loss, jitter) by exchanging authenticated PROBE
frames, and drives liveness/failover decisions. Carries the anti-replay
primitives (`AntiReplay` high-water, `ControlGuard`) that protect PROBE and
CONTROL against replay of captured valid-MAC frames.

**GUARD (must-hold invariant).** A **single-endpoint** list takes **no** failover
action — no advance, no remote repoint, no re-handshake. A one-concentrator
deployment (including the legacy single `endpoint` form, normalized to a
one-element list) is therefore byte-for-byte the pre-T57 behaviour. The switch and
this guard are validated by the real-network netns e2e (`TestHubFailoverStandbySwitch`
+ `TestHubFailoverSingleEndpointGuard`, T62) and, over the real internet, by the
realhosts mid-transfer WAN-kill tier (`TestRealMidTransferWANKill`, T63).

**Re-resolution** (`resolution`, T73). A hostname endpoint spec has no fixed
address; its expansion is a **mutable, spec-keyed record set** the failover set
carries (`failoverSpec.addrs`, updated in place by `hubFailover.updateResolution`
under the endpoint-set lock — the sole mutation point). The re-resolution
controller keeps those record sets fresh. It mirrors the `hubFailover` shape (a pure
constructor over an injected `dnsresolve.Resolver`, the failover controller it
drives, a `telemetry.Clock`, and the `[dns]` poll interval + per-lookup timeout) and
runs its own device-lifecycle loop **off the send hot path** — all lookups happen on
its goroutine; results are applied only through `updateResolution`. Each evaluation:

- **Poll** every hostname spec on the fixed `[dns]` cadence; on a **successful,
  non-empty** lookup the addrs are family-ordered (IPv4 first, then IPv6, deduped —
  a deterministic order so an unchanged answer yields a byte-identical expansion) and
  handed to `updateResolution`, which **repoints only on an actual active-IP change**
  (D32 no-op suppression). When the transport exposes a TTL (DoH/DoT), the next poll
  is clamped to `min(pollInterval, minTTL)`.
- **Liveness-loss trigger**: the instant every path to the **active** endpoint reads
  `StateDown` — the *same* `allDown` sweep the failover loop advances on (Q34: the
  two controllers coordinate purely through the shared lock and the update API) — the
  active spec is re-resolved **out of band**, edge-triggered, without waiting for the
  next poll tick.
- **Retention invariant (D46)**: a lookup that **fails** (error/timeout/NXDOMAIN) or
  yields an **empty** set **never publishes** — the spec keeps its last-good
  expansion and the controller retries next tick. A transient resolver fault
  therefore never tears down a working endpoint set, and `hubFailover` never sees a
  previously-resolved active spec collapse to empty (the condition its `total < 2`
  guard could otherwise strand the bond on).

This controller runs **even for a single-hostname peer** (to track a changing DDNS
address), independent of hub-failover's `>= 2` guard; the first successful poll of a
hostname-only peer is what boot-adopts its active endpoint.
